title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals depend three elements: cdf bootstrap replications ti*t_i^*, =1…Bi=1\ldots B bias-correction number z0=Φ(∑iBI(ti*<t0)/B)z_0 = \Phi(\sum_i^B (t_i^* < t_0) / B ) t0=f(x)t_0=f(x) original estimate acceleration number aa measures rate change σt0\sigma_{t_0} xx, data changes. first two depend bootstrap distribution, generated: parametrically non-parametrically. Package bcaboot aims make construction bootstrap confidence intervals almost automatic. three main functions user : bcajack bcajack2 nonparametric bootstrap bcapar parametric bootstrap details Efron Narasimhan (2018) paper. Much theory behind approach can found references Efron (1987), DiCiccio Efron (1992), DiCiccio Efron (1996), Efron Hastie (2016).

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Suppose wish construct bootstrap confidence intervals R2R^2-statistic linear regression. Using diabetes data lars (442 11) example, use function regress y x, matrix 10 predictors, compute R2R^2. Constructing bootstrap confidence intervals involves merely calling bcajack: result contains several components. confidence interval limits can obtained via first column shows estimated Bca confidence limits requested alpha percentiles can compared standard limits θ±σ̂zα\theta \pm \hat{\sigma}z_{\alpha} column titled standard. jacksd column jacksd gives internal standard errors Bca limits, quite small example. pct column gives percentiles ordered B bootstrap replications corresponding Bca limits, e.g. 91.85 percentile equals .975 Bca limit .5600968. details provided stats component. first column theta original point estimate parameter interest, sdboot bootstrap estimate standard error. quantity z0 Bca bias correction value, case quite negative; acceleration, component Bca limits (nearly zero ). Finally, sdjack jackknife estimate standard error theta. bottom line gives internal standard errors five quantities . substantial z0 . component ustats result provides bias-corrected estimator estimate sampling error. resulting object can plotted using bcaplot.
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {     y <- Xy[, 11]     X <- Xy[, 1:10]     summary(lm(y ~ X) )$adj.r.squared } set.seed(1234) result <- bcajack(x = Xy, B = 2000, func = rfun, verbose = FALSE) knitr::kable(result$lims, digits = 3) knitr::kable(result$stats, digits = 3) knitr::kable(t(result$ustats), digits = 3) bcaplot(result)

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: logistic regression fit data 812 neonates large clinic. summary dataset. goal predict death versus survival—yy 1 0, respectively—basis 11 baseline variables one resp particular concern. (207 deaths 605 survivors.) θ\theta, parameter interest coefficient resp. Discussions investigator suggested weighting 4 1 deaths versus non-deaths.

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Parametric bootstrapping context requires us independently sample response according estimated probabilities regression model. discussed paper accompanying software, routine bcapar also requires sufficient statistics β̂=M′y\hat{\beta} = M^\prime y MM model matrix. Therefore, makes sense function work. function glm_boot returns list estimate θ̂\hat{\theta}, bootstrap estimates, sufficient statistics. Now can compute bootstrap estimates using bcapar. can examine bootstrap limits statistics. bootstrap standard error using B=2000B=2000 samples resp can read last table 0.943±0.1550.943\pm 0.155. can also see small upward bias fact 0.585 proportion bootstrap replicates 0.9430.943. also reflected bias-corrector term ẑ0=−0.215\hat{z}_0= -0.215 table internal standard error $0.024.
code: weights <- with(neonates, ifelse(y == 0, 1, 4)) glm_model <- glm(formula = y ~ ., family = "binomial", weights = weights, data = neonates) summary(glm_model) ##  ## Call: ## glm(formula = y ~ ., family = "binomial", data = neonates, weights = weights) ##  ## Coefficients: ##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     ## (Intercept) -0.26510    0.07598  -3.489 0.000485 *** ## gest        -0.70602    0.13117  -5.383 7.35e-08 *** ## ap          -0.78594    0.07874  -9.982  < 2e-16 *** ## bwei        -0.23332    0.12592  -1.853 0.063879 .   ## gen         -0.04107    0.07355  -0.558 0.576594     ## resp         0.94306    0.08974  10.509  < 2e-16 *** ## head         0.04813    0.08057   0.597 0.550299     ## hr           0.03504    0.07191   0.487 0.626045     ## cpap         0.43438    0.08869   4.898 9.70e-07 *** ## age          0.15727    0.08492   1.852 0.064041 .   ## temp        -0.05960    0.08506  -0.701 0.483520     ## size        -0.37477    0.09919  -3.778 0.000158 *** ## --- ## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ##  ## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) ##  ##     Null deviance: 1951.7  on 811  degrees of freedom ## Residual deviance: 1187.2  on 800  degrees of freedom ## AIC: 1211.2 ##  ## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5 glm_boot <- function(B, glm_model, weights, var = "resp") {     pi_hat <- glm_model$fitted.values     n <- length(pi_hat)     y_star <- sapply(seq_len(B), function(i) ifelse(runif(n) <= pi_hat, 1, 0))     beta_star <- apply(y_star, 2, function(y) {         boot_data <- glm_model$data         boot_data$y <- y         coef(glm(formula = y ~ ., data = boot_data, weights = weights, family = "binomial"))     })     list(theta = coef(glm_model)[var],          theta_star = beta_star[var, ],          suff_stat = t(y_star) %*% model.matrix(glm_model)) } set.seed(3891) glm_boot_out <- glm_boot(B = 2000, glm_model = glm_model, weights = weights) glm_bca <- bcapar(t0 = glm_boot_out$theta,                   tt = glm_boot_out$theta_star,                   bb = glm_boot_out$suff_stat) knitr::kable(glm_bca$lims, digits = 3) knitr::kable(glm_bca$stats, digits = 3)

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Now suppose wish use nonstandard estimation procedure, example, via glmnet package, uses cross-validation figure best fit, corresponding penalization parameter λ\lambda (named lambda.min). can examine estimates lambda.min follows. Following lines , create helper function perform bootstrap. go. can compare output got glm . can examine bootstrap limits statistics. shrinkage evident; now bootstrap estimate now 0.862±0.1270.862\pm 0.127. fact, now 0.339 proportion bootstrap replicates 0.8620.862. Therefore, bias corrector large: ẑ0=0.411.\hat{z}_0 = 0.411. Finally, can plot glm glmnet results side--side.
code: X <- as.matrix(neonates[, seq_len(11)]) ; Y <- neonates$y; glmnet_model <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(x = X, y = Y, family = "binomial", weights = weights) coefs <- as.matrix(coef(glmnet_model, s = glmnet_model$lambda.min)) knitr::kable(data.frame(variable = rownames(coefs), coefficient = coefs[, 1]), row.names = FALSE, digits = 3) glmnet_boot <- function(B, X, y, glmnet_model, weights, var = "resp") {     lambda <- glmnet_model$lambda.min     theta <- as.matrix(coef(glmnet_model, s = lambda))     pi_hat <- predict(glmnet_model, newx = X, s = "lambda.min", type = "response")     n <- length(pi_hat)     y_star <- sapply(seq_len(B), function(i) ifelse(runif(n) <= pi_hat, 1, 0))     beta_star <- apply(y_star, 2,                        function(y) {                            as.matrix(coef(glmnet::glmnet(x = X, y = y, lambda = lambda, weights = weights, family = "binomial")))                        })      rownames(beta_star) <- rownames(theta)     list(theta = theta[var, ],          theta_star = beta_star[var, ],          suff_stat = t(y_star) %*% X) } glmnet_boot_out <- glmnet_boot(B = 2000, X, y, glmnet_model, weights) glmnet_bca <- bcapar(t0 = glmnet_boot_out$theta,                      tt = glmnet_boot_out$theta_star,                      bb = glmnet_boot_out$suff_stat) knitr::kable(glmnet_bca$lims, digits = 3) knitr::kable(glmnet_bca$stats, digits = 3)

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Assume two independent estimates variance normal theory: σ̂12∼σ12χn12n1, \hat{\sigma}_1^2\sim\frac{\sigma_1^2\chi_{n_1}^2}{n_1}, σ̂22∼σ22χn22n2. \hat{\sigma}_2^2\sim\frac{\sigma_2^2\chi_{n_2}^2}{n_2}. Suppose now parameter interest θ=σ12σ22     \theta=\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2} wish compute confidence limits. setting, theory yields exact limits: θ̂(α)=θ̂Fn1,n21−α. \hat{\theta}(\alpha) = \frac{\hat{\theta}}{F_{n_1,n_2}^{1-\alpha}}. can apply bcapar problem. , helper functions. Note additional function funcF corresponds τ(β̂*)\tau(\hat{\beta}^*) paper. function expressing parameter interest function sample. limits obtained shown , along exact limits last column. Clearly bca limits match exact values well suggests large upward correction standard limits. corrections positive seen table ; ẑ0=0.093\hat{z}_0 = 0.093 ̂=0.092\hat{} = 0.092. plot shows moderate amount internal error θ̂bca(0.975)\hat{\theta}_{bca}(0.975) shown red bar. pct column suggests : θ̂bca(0.975)\hat{\theta}_{bca}(0.975) occurs 0.9960.996-quantile 16,000 replications, .e., 64th largest θ̂\hat{\theta}, limited amount data estimating distribution.
code: ratio_boot <- function(B, v1, v2) {     s1 <- sqrt(v1) * rchisq(n = B, df = n1)  / n1     s2 <- sqrt(v2) * rchisq(n = B, df = n2)  / n2     theta_star <- s1 / s2     beta_star <- cbind(s1, s2)     list(theta = v1 / v2,          theta_star = theta_star,          suff_stat = beta_star) }  funcF <- function(beta) {     beta[1] / beta[2] } B <- 16000; n1 <- 10; n2 <- 42 ratio_boot_out <- ratio_boot(B, 1, 1) ratio_bca <- bcapar(t0 = ratio_boot_out$theta,                  tt = ratio_boot_out$theta_star,                  bb = ratio_boot_out$suff_stat, func = funcF) exact <- 1 / qf(df1 = n1, df2 = n2, p = 1 - as.numeric(rownames(ratio_bca$lims))) knitr::kable(cbind(ratio_bca$lims, exact = exact), digits = 3) knitr::kable(ratio_bca$stats, digits = 3) knitr::kable(t(ratio_bca$abcstats), digits = 3) knitr::kable(ratio_bca$ustats, digits = 3) bcaplot(ratio_bca)

text: Bootstrap confidence intervals depend three elements: () cumulative distribution bootstrap replications, (b) bias-correction, (c) acceleration number measures rate change standard deviation estimate data changes.  first two depend bootstrap distribution, generated: parametrically non-parametrically. Therefore, difference parametric bca analysis lie nonparametric estimation acceleration, often negligible error.

code: bcajack(   x,   B,   func,   ...,   m = nrow(x),   mr = 5,   K = 2,   J = 10,   alpha = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
text: x \(n \times p\) data matrix, rows observed \(p\)-vectors, assumed independently sampled target population. \(p\) 1 x can vector. B number bootstrap replications. can also vector B bootstrap replications estimated parameter interest, computed separately. func function \(\hat{\theta}=func(x)\) computing estimate parameter interest; \(func(x)\) return real value \(n^\prime \times p\) matrix \(x^\prime\), \(n^\prime\) necessarily equal \(n\) ... additional arguments func. m integer less equal \(n\); routine collects \(n\) rows x m groups speed jackknife calculations estimating acceleration value \(\); typically m 20 40 exactly divide \(n\). However, warnings shown. mr \(m < n\) mr repetions randomly grouped jackknife calculations averaged. K non-negative integer. K > 0, bcajack also returns estimates internal standard error, , variability due stopping B bootstrap replications rather going infinity. obtained second type jackknifing, taking average K separate jackknife estimates, randomly splitting B bootstrap replications J groups. J number groups bootstrap replications split alpha percentiles desired bca confidence limits. One needs provide alpha values 0.5; upper limits automatically computed verbose logical verbose progress messages

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
text: named list several items lims : first column shows estimated bca confidence limits requested alpha percentiles. can compared standard limits \(\hat{\theta} +     \hat{\sigma}z_{\alpha}\), third column. second column jacksd gives internal standard errors bca limits, quite small example. Column 4, pct, gives percentiles ordered B bootstrap replications corresponding bca limits, eg 897th largest replication equalling .975 bca limit .557. stats : top line stats shows 5 estimates: theta \(f(x)\), original point estimate parameter interest; sdboot bootstrap estimate standard error; z0 bca bias correction value, case quite negative; acceleration, component bca limits (nearly zero ); sdjack jackknife estimate standard error theta. Bottom line gives internal standard errors five quantities . substantial z0 . B.mean : bootstrap sample size B, mean B bootstrap replications \(\hat{\theta^*}\) ustats : bias-corrected estimator 2 * t0 - mean(tt), estimate sdu sampling error seed : random number state reproducibility

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals depend three elements: cdf \(B\) bootstrap replications \(t_i^*\), \(=1\ldots B\) bias-correction number \(z_0=\Phi(\sum_i^B (t_i^* < t_0) / B )\) \(t_0=f(x)\) original estimate acceleration number \(\) measures rate change \(\sigma_{t_0}\) \(x\), data changes. first two depend bootstrap distribution, generated: parametrically non-parametrically. Program bcajack can used hybrid fashion vector tt B bootstrap replications first generated parametric model. , diabetes example , might first draw bootstrap samples \(y^* \sim N(X\hat{\beta}, \hat{\sigma}^2 )\) \(\hat{\beta}\) \(\hat{\sigma}\) obtained lm(y~X); \(y^*\) provide bootstrap replication tstar = rfun(cbind(X, ystar)).  get bca intervals bcajack(Xy, tt, rfun ....) tt, vector B tstar values. difference full parametric bca analysis lie nonparametric estimation \(\), often negligible error.

title: Nonparametric bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap confidence limits — bcajack2
text: function version bcajack allows recomputations original statistic function \(f\) carried separately. advantage \(f\) time-consuming, case B replications nonparametric bca calculations might need done distributed basis. use bcajack2 mode, first compute list Blist via Blist <- list(Y = Y, tt = tt, t0 = t0).  tt vector length B -th entry tt[] <- func(x[Ii,], ...), x \(n \times p\) data matrix Ii bootstrap vector (observation) indices. Y B \(n\) count matrix, whose -th row counts corresponding Ii. example n = 5 Ii = (2, 5, 2, 1, 4), Yi = (1, 2, 0, 1, 1). computed Blist, bcajack2 invoked bcajack2(Blist) without need enter function \(func\).

B Blist explained , x needed. func function \(\hat{\theta}=func(x)\) computing estimate parameter interest; \(func(x)\) return real value \(n^\prime \times p\) matrix \(x^\prime\), \(n^\prime\) necessarily equal \(n\) ... additional arguments func. m integer less equal \(n\); routine collects \(n\) rows x m groups speed jackknife calculations estimating acceleration value \(\); typically m 20 40 exactly divide \(n\). However, warnings shown. mr \(m < n\) mr repetions randomly grouped jackknife calculations averaged. pct bcajack2 uses count vectors nearest (1,1,...1) estimate gradient statistic, "nearest" defined count vectors smallest pct B . Default value `pct 1/3 (see appendix Efron Narasimhan details) K non-negative integer. K > 0, bcajack also returns estimates internal standard error, , variability due stopping B bootstrap replications rather going infinity. obtained second type jackknifing, taking average K separate jackknife estimates, randomly splitting B bootstrap replications J groups. J number groups bootstrap replications split alpha percentiles desired bca confidence limits. One needs provide alpha values 0.5; upper limits automatically computed verbose logical verbose progress messages

Column 4, pct, gives percentiles ordered B bootstrap replications corresponding bca limits, eg 897th largest replication equalling .975 bca limit .557. stats : top line stats shows 5 estimates: theta \(func(x)\), original point estimate parameter interest; sdboot bootstrap estimate standard error; z0 bca bias correction value, case quite negative; acceleration, component bca limits (nearly zero ); sdjack jackknife estimate standard error theta. what: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
title: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
text: function computes nonparametric BCa confidence intervals using regression bootstrap count vectors (approach bcajack2) plus generalized BCa (gbca) diagnostic tests adequacy BCa approximation via warped normal comparison. Efron (1987) Sec 7.

title: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
code: bcanon(   B,   x,   func,   ...,   m = nrow(x),   pct = 0.333,   K = 2,   J = 12,   alpha = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
text: B number bootstrap replications, list components Y (count matrix), tt (bootstrap replicates), t0 (original estimate) x data matrix dimension \(n\times p\), rows assumed mutually independent func R function, \(func(x, \ldots)\) statistic interest ... additional arguments func m number units; \(m<n\) original units collected groups size \(n/m\); useful \(n\) large pct proportion nearby count vectors used finding gradient K number jackknife repetitions internal standard error estimation J number jackknife folds per repetition alpha percentiles coverage probabilities bootstrap intervals verbose logical verbose progress messages

title: Compute nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bcanon
text: named list class bcaboot containing: call: matched call lims : BCa confidence limits (bca), jackknife internal standard errors (jacksd), standard limits (std), bootstrap percentiles (pct) stats : Estimates jackknife Monte Carlo errors theta, sdboot, sdjack, z0, , plus big-acceleration SE stability (, sese) B.mean : bootstrap sample size B mean replications equiv : gbca coverage estimates showing alpha vs alphatilda (coverage-adjusted alpha); BCa exact equal. Also jackknife Monte Carlo standard deviations. seed : random number state reproducibility

what: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
text: bcapar computes parametric bootstrap confidence intervals real-valued parameter theta p-parameter exponential family. described Section 4 reference .

title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
text: t0 Observed estimate theta, usually maximum likelihood. tt vector parametric bootstrap replications theta length B, usually large, say B = 2000 bb B p matrix natural sufficient vectors, p dimension exponential family. alpha percentiles desired bca confidence limits. One needs provide alpha values 0.5; upper limits automatically computed J, K Parameters controlling jackknife estimates Monte Carlo error: J jackknife folds, jackknife standard errors averaged K random divisions bb trun Truncation parameter used calculation acceleration . pct Proportion "nearby" b vectors used calculation t., gradient vector theta. cd cd 1 bca confidence density also returned; see Section 11.6 reference Efron Hastie (2016) func Function \(\hat{\theta} = func(b)\). missing output includes abc estimates; see reference DiCiccio Efron (1992)

title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
text: named list several items: lims : Bca confidence limits (first column) standard limits (fourth column). Also abc limits (fifth column) func provided. second column, jacksd, jackknife estimates Monte Carlo error; pct, third column proportion replicates tt less bcalim value stats : Estimates jackknife Monte Carlo errors: theta = \(\hat{\theta}\); sd, bootstrap standard deviation \(\hat{\theta}\); acceleration estimate; az another acceleration estimate depends less extreme values tt; z0 bias-correction estimate; big-measure raw acceleration; sdd delta method estimate standard deviation \(\hat{\theta}\); mean average tt abcstats : abc estimates z0, returned func provided ustats : bias-corrected estimator 2 * t0 - mean(tt). ustats gives ustat, estimate sdu sampling error, jackknife estimates monte carlo error ustat sdu. Also given B, number bootstrap replications seed : random number state reproducibility

title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar
text: DiCiccio T Efron B (1996). Bootstrap confidence intervals. Statistical Science 11, 189-228 T. DiCiccio B. Efron. accurate confidence intervals exponential families. Biometrika (1992) p231-245. Efron B (1987). Better bootstrap confidence intervals. JASA 82, 171-200 B. Efron T. Hastie. Computer Age Statistical Inference. Cambridge University Press, 2016. B. Efron B. Narasimhan. Automatic Construction Bootstrap Confidence Intervals, 2018.

title: Compute parametric bootstrap confidence intervals — bcapar

what: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
title: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
text: bcaplot uses output bcajack, bcajack2, bcanon, bcapar plot bca standard confidence limits parameter interest.

title: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
code: bcaplot(   vl,   main = " ",   xlab = "coverage",   ylab = "limits",   alpha = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16),   ylim,   xlim,   add = 0,   sub = "black=bca, green=standard",   sw = 1,   ... )

title: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
text: vl output bcajack, bcajack2, bcanon, bcapar main main caption (can empty) xlab x axis label (supplied specified) ylab y axis labels (supplied specified) alpha Coverages \(1-2\alpha\), e.g. alpha=c(.025,.05) plots intervals [.025,.975] [.05,.95]. Default alpha=c(.025,.05,.1,.16) giving coverages .95,.90,.80,.68 ylim y axis plot limits set automatically provided xlim x axis plot limits set automatically provided add add=1 adds new plot bca limits (red) existing plot sub subtitle (can empty) sw sw=1 draws light vertical dashed lines showing bca intervals ... args plot

title: Plots of bca confidence limits — bcaplot
text: confidence interval endpoints plotted vertically versus two-sided coverages \(1-2\alpha\). Bca limits black, Standard limits green (dashed.). vl$lims includes column "jacksd" jackknife internal standard deviations indicated vertical red bars centered bca limit points.

what: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
title: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
text: BCa percentile formula: maps nominal alpha levels bias--acceleration adjusted percentiles bootstrap distribution. Efron (1987) Sec 2.

title: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
text: z0 Bias-correction constant (scalar). Acceleration constant (scalar). zalpha Normal quantiles desired alpha levels (vector). boot_reps Sorted vector B bootstrap replicates.

title: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
text: Named list limits (BCa quantiles), bca_idx (indices sorted boot_reps), iles (adjusted percentile levels).

text: row Y bootstrap count vector. deviance measures far bootstrap sample departs simple random sampling (uniform counts). Limit Yi=0: term equals 2. EN20 appendix.

text: Y B x m count matrix (rows = bootstrap samples, cols = observation counts).

title: Compute Poisson/multinomial deviance from uniform for bootstrap count matrix. — compute_kl_distance
text: Numeric vector length B deviance values.
